coredata.dc:title: Facile preparation of fluorescent N, S co-doped carbon dots for the sensitive detection of tetracycline and Al3+
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Xiaoxu
Wan, Zherui
Li, Zhonghao
Wang, Fengxia
Wei, Pengshuai
Yang, Guang
coredata.dc:description: Herein, intelligent N, S-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N, S-CDs) were constructed by using l-cysteine and carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) polymer as precursors through a one-step hydrothermal method. The designed N, S-CDs displayed strong fluorescence (FL) emission with a FL quantum yield of 44.48 % and could be utilized for the quantitative tracing of TCs and Al3+ via the “On-OFF-ON” FL transition with wide linear detection range (1.66–104.5 μM for TCH, 1.66–163.2 μM for OTC, 1.66–69.5 μM for DOX and 33–104.3 μM for Al3+). The detection limits (LODs) of TCH, OTC, DOX and Al3+ were calculated to be 53.02 nM, 95.61 nM, 41.87 nM and 57.69 nM, respectively. On the basis of the FL “On-OFF-ON” strategy, multiple logical gate operations including “NOR” and “INHIBIT”, were constructed successfully. Additionally, N, S-CDs uploaded to hydrogen bars were utilized for the recognition of TCs and Al3+ in a portable manner, which revealed great application potential in the field of anti-counterfeiting and encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N, S-doped carbon dots
Fluorescent sensor
TCs and Al3+ detection
Anti-counterfeiting
Logic gates

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Calculation of the radiation rate (Vr) and non-radiation rate (Vnr)
Calculation of the quenching constant (Ksv) and binding association constant (Ka)
Preparation of the fluorescent hydrogel for anti-counterfeiting encryption
Optical properties of the N, S-CDs
FL sensing of TCs by N, S-CDs
FL detection mechanism for TCH and Al3+
Real sample detection of TCH and Al3+
Portable detection of TCH and Al3+ for anti-counterfeiting encryption

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The TEM image and particle size distribution, (b) EDS spectrum, (c) XRD spectrum, and (d) Raman spectrum of the N, S-CDs.
(a) The FT-IR spectrum, (b) XPS spectrum and the high-resolution XPS spectra of (c) C1s, (d) N1s, (e) O1s and (f) S2p spectrum of the N, S-CDs.

(a) The UV–vis spectrum (black curve), FL excitation spectrum (blue curve) and emission spectrum (red curve) of N, S-CDs (inset photos of the N, S-CDs under daylight (left) and 365 nm light irradiation (right), and (b) FL emission spectra of N, S- CDs with excitation wavelengths varies from 300 nm to 400 nm.
(a) FL spectra of the N, S-CDs upon in the presence different amounts of TCH and (b) the linear relationships between lg (F0/F) of N, S-CDs at 425 nm and TCH amounts.
The FL intensities of the N, S-CDs at 425 nm in the presence of other different (a) organic molecules and (b) metal ions (104.5 μM).
(a) FL spectra of the N, S-CDs + TCH upon in the presence different amounts of Al3+ and (b) the linear relationships between lg (F0/F) of N, S-CDs and Al3+ amounts.
The photographs and fluorescent images of the N, S-CDs based hydrogel (a) without information storage, (b) with the treatment with TCH, and (c) with the treatment with Al3+ once again.
Truth tables for the (a) NOR gate and (b) INHIBIT gate operation for the N, S-CDs.
Schematic illustration for the preparation of N, S-CDs as a sensitive FL sensor for the detection of TCs and Al3+ with FL “on-off-on” transition.

head.ce:title: Facile preparation of fluorescent N, S co-doped carbon dots for the sensitive detection of tetracycline and Al3+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Northeast Forestry University, Hexing Road 26, Harbin, 150040, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Northeast Forestry University, Hexing Road 26, Harbin, 150040, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, intelligent N, S-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N, S-CDs) were constructed by using l-cysteine and carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) polymer as precursors through a one-step hydrothermal method. The designed N, S-CDs displayed strong fluorescence (FL) emission with a FL quantum yield of 44.48 % and could be utilized for the quantitative tracing of TCs and Al3+ via the “On-OFF-ON” FL transition with wide linear detection range (1.66–104.5 μM for TCH, 1.66–163.2 μM for OTC, 1.66–69.5 μM for DOX and 33–104.3 μM for Al3+). The detection limits (LODs) of TCH, OTC, DOX and Al3+ were calculated to be 53.02 nM, 95.61 nM, 41.87 nM and 57.69 nM, respectively. On the basis of the FL “On-OFF-ON” strategy, multiple logical gate operations including “NOR” and “INHIBIT”, were constructed successfully. Additionally, N, S-CDs uploaded to hydrogen bars were utilized for the recognition of TCs and Al3+ in a portable manner, which revealed great application potential in the field of anti-counterfeiting and encryption.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As important antibiotics, tetracyclines (TCs) are widely employed in food and medicine because of their outstanding antibacterial abilities and low price. However, overuse of TCs such as tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH), oxytetracycline (OTC) and doxycycline (DOX) can accumulate in the human body via the consumption process, which causes great threat to human health [1–3]. To date, several well-established analytical methods, including capillary electrophoresis (CE), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), mass spectrometry (MS) and colorimetric or electrochemical analysis, have been reported for TCs detection with high sensitivity and accuracy [4–10]. However, the requirements of expensive equipment, complex sample preparation processes and skilled technicians limit their further practical application. To protect human health, developing effective methods with fast, sensitive and convenient properties for TCs detection is essential.

Compared with the above traditional analytical methods, the FL analytical technique has gained considerable interest because of its simple and convenient operation, low cost, fast response and excellent specificity with higher accuracy, which makes it become a preferred platform for TCs detection. Currently, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have gained extensive attention because of their fast preparation, intense FL emission, low toxicity and excellent hydrophilicity, and have been widely investigated as fluorescent sensors for the detection of TCs [11–24].

To increase the FL detection ability of CDs, the doping of heteroatoms or metal atoms is believed to be an effective strategy. For example, Yan et al. prepared N-CDs with yellow-green FL emission at room temperature by using o-phenylenediamine and glyoxal via the amine-aldehyde condensation reaction. The FL of the designed N-CDs was quenched in the presence of TC by the inner filter effect (IFE), and the limit of detection (LOD) of TC was 6.0 μM [11]. Zhao et al. synthesized N-CDs upon using ethylenediamine and puffer fish skin as precursors via a hydrothermal method, and the as-prepared fluorescent sensor could be utilized for the detection of TCs through a FL quenching strategy [12]. Meanwhile, an Al-CD-based fluorescent sensing platform with bright blue FL emission was designed by Xu and coworkers via a one-step hydrothermal method, which displayed sensitive detection of five kinds of TCs simultaneously [13]. Moreover, Li et al. constructed a ratiometric FL sensor of Zn-CDs through simple hydrothermal treatment with ZnCl2 and citrate. The FL emission of the Zn-CDs at 440 nm was quenched, and the FL emission at 515 nm was enhanced after the addition of TCH with a lower LOD of 61.1 nM [14]. Meanwhile, several works have demonstrated that the combination of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) could increase the detection ability of TCs. For instance, a novel FL probe of UiO-67-NCDs was reported by Zhang et al. via the combination of N-CDs and UiO-67 through hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions. The UiO-67-NCDs displayed increased sensitivity and fast response toward TC detection through the FL quenching process with an LOD of 32.9 nM [15]. Li et al. fabricated a ratiometric-sensing platform (ZIF/CDs) for tracing TC via the modification of N-CDs with ZIF-8. Interestingly, an increase in the FL of the ZIF/CDs at 516 nm was observed through the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effect, and the FL at 466 nm remained unchanged after the addition of TC, which could be used for the quantitative analysis of TC with excellent specificity and high accuracy [16]. Although many CD-based fluorescent sensors have been introduced for the analysis of TCs with easy operation and high sensitivity, the majority of reported works for the tracing of TCs have focused mainly on an FL “ON-OFF” strategy, and the concept of whether the quenched system can be reused as another sensing platform for detecting other substances by the FL “OFF-ON” process has rarely been reported [25,26]. Therefore, the development of smart multifunctional CDs as efficient and sensitive sensors for detecting TCs and other toxic substances through the FL “ON-OFF-ON” strategy is highly important in the fields of health and environmental protection.

On the basis of the above considerations, N, S co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) with blue emission were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal treatment with l-cysteine and sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) polymer. The blue FL emission of the N, S-CDs was sharply quenched by the treatment with TCs through the inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching processes. Interestingly, the quenched system exhibited obvious FL recovery after treatment with Al3+ due to the greater combination of Al3+ and TCs. Thus, a smart sensor with an FL “ON-OFF-ON” transition was constructed for the sensitive and selective detection of TCs and Al3+. Moreover, the designed sensing platform has been applied for anti-counterfeiting encryption, logic gate operations and real sample detection of TCs and Al3+.

Sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC, viscosity: 600–3000 mPa s), potassium persulfate (KSP), acrylamide (AM), and N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA) were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. l-cysteine, D- or l-histidine, D- or l-tyrosine and other amino acids or other inorganic salts were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai) and deionized water was used during the experiment.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained via an FEI TECNAI G2 transmission electron microscope produced by Philips Netherlands. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained using an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were captured using a Spectrum 400 spectrometer from PerkinElmer USA, and the Raman spectra were acquired via a DXR2 confocal Raman microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were recorded using an XRD-6100 instrument from Shimadzu Japan. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the sample was recorded using a Shimadzu UV-2550 PC UV–visible spectrophotometer, and FL data were obtained using an LS-55 FL spectrometer from PerkinElmer USA. The FL lifetime was recorded using an FLS980 spectrometer from Edinburgh Instruments UK.

The synthetic route of the proposed N, S-CDs was similar to the previously reported work [16]. Briefly, 0.20 g of CMC and 0.075 g of l-cysteine were added to 15 mL of deionized water, and the mixture was then transferred into a dry dried Teflon lined autoclave (20 mL) and reacted at 200 °C for 240 min. The mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, yielding a brown-yellow solution. Finally, the solution was freeze-dried for 48 h to obtain the orange-yellow N, S-CDs powder with a yield of 68 %. The obtained N, S-CDs powder were added into a centrifuge tube that sealed with sealing film, and stored at a glass desiccator for further use.

The QY of the N, S-CDs was calculated via the use of quinine sulfate as a reference, employing the following formula:
Q Y =
Q R
(
I / I
R
)
(
O D
R
/ O D
)
(
n 2
/
n R 2
)
, which was similar to that used in our previous works [27,28]. Herein, “I” refers to the measured integrated FL intensity, “OD” is the optical density measured via a UV–vis spectroscopy, “n” stands for the refractive index of the solvent (1.33) and the QR is the quantum yield of quinine sulfate (54 %). The subscript “R” represents the fluorescent standard sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of the radiation rate (Vr) and non-radiation rate (Vnr)
ce:section.ce:para:
The radiation (Vr) and non-radiation (Vnr) attenuation rates are calculated based on the following formulas [29]:
τ = 1 /
(
V r
+
V
n r
)
Q Y =
V r
(
V r
+
V
n r
)
Herein, τ is the FL lifetime and the QY represents the quantum yield of the N, S-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of the quenching constant (Ksv) and binding association constant (Ka)
ce:section.ce:para:
The Ksv is calculated via the Stern-Volmer equation by the following formula [14]:
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
s v
[ C ]
= 1 +
K q
τ
a v
[ C ]

The Ka is calculated by the following formula [30]:
lg
F o
− F
F
= lg
K a
+ n
lg
[ C ]

where F0 and F are the FL intensities before and after the treatment with TCs, respectively. C represents the concentration of TCs, K
q is the quenching dynamic quenching rate constant and τ
av represents the average FL lifetime of the N, S-CDs (1.02 ns), the detail calculation of τ
av was illustrated in Table S1.

Briefly, different concentrations of TCs including TCH, OTC and DOX were added to 3 mL of N, S-CDs solution (pH = 5) with an incubation time of 30 s, and the FL spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 335 nm. The FL emission intensity at 425 nm was utilized for quantitative analysis.

The real water samples including tap water and Songhua River water were filtered through a 0.22 μm Millipore filter, and then different amounts of TCs were added to the above sample with an incubation time of 30 s. The FL intensity at 425 nm was recorded (λex: 335 nm, slit width: 5 nm) for quantitative analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the fluorescent hydrogel for anti-counterfeiting encryption
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent hydrogels containing N, S-CDs were obtained through free radical polymerization [31]. Briefly, 2 g of AM and 1.5 mL of N, S-CDs solution were added to 20 mL of deionized water, followed by ultrasonic treatment for 60 s. Then, 0.25 g of MBA and 0.125 g of KSP were added to the mixture and transferred into the mold immediately. Polymerization was achieved at 60 °C for 30 min to obtain the fluorescent hydrogel. Finally, filter papers with different shapes contained TCH or Al3+ were labeled on hydrogel rods for 0.5 h to form hydrogel rods with different patterns, which were utilized for anti-counterfeiting encryption.

The N, S-CDs were obtained through a one-step hydrothermal method using l-cysteine (L-Cys) and CMC polymer as precursors (Scheme 1 ). The optimal conditions, such as the reactant ratio, reaction time and temperature were investigated via QY values and the corresponding results were illustrated in Table S2 and S3 and S4. The above studies revealed that the N, S-CDs prepared with a reactant ratio of.

CMC: L-Cys = 0.2:0.075 (g) at a reaction temperature of 200 °C for 4 h displayed excellent FL properties. The TEM image in Fig. 1 a shows that the N, S-CDs had a spherical-like structure with a uniform distribution, and the average diameter was approximately 4.95 nm (inset of Fig. 1a). The energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) data in Fig. 1b show that four elements of C, N, O and S were present in the N, S-CDs, indicating the successful doping of N and S. The XRD pattern of the N, S-CDs in Fig. 1c reveals a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 20.8o, which originated from the graphite plane (002) [20]. Furthermore, the Raman spectrum of the N, S-CDs in Fig. 1d revealed two distinct peaks located at 1343 and 1575 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G bands, respectively. The D band stands for the structural disorder of the sp2 domain and the G band represents the graphite.

phase. Notably, the intensity ratio ID/IG was calculated to be 1.04, which also demonstrated that the tiny graphitized phase was embedded in the discorded carbon in the N, S-CDs with more defects [32,33].

In addition, the functional units of the N, S-CDs were investigated by FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the peaks at 3373 and 3165 cm−1 were verified as the overlapping stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H [14,19]. The peaks at 2921 and 2835 cm−1 were considered to be the stretching vibrations of C–H and –CH2, respectively [34]. Moreover, the characteristic peaks at 1688, 1584, 1394 and 1278 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of CO, CC, C–H and C–N, respectively. The characteristic peak at 1094 cm−1 was attributed to the C–O–C stretching vibration, whereas the typical peak located at 669 cm−1 was considered to be the C–S stretching vibration [29,35]. The FT-IR analysis indicated that the N, S-CDs contained the water soluble functional groups of –OH and –NH2, and the S was successfully doped into the N, S-CDs. Furthermore, XPS spectral analysis was performed to further evaluate the elemental composition and chemical structure of the N, S-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2b, the full scan XPS spectrum of the N, S-CDs mainly contained four elements of C, N, O and S, with the atomic percentages of 52.57 %, 4.55 %, 39.27 %, and 3.62 %, respectively. The characteristic peaks at 285.0 eV, 400.0 eV, 531.0 eV, 227.2 eV and 163.5 eV were regarded as the C1s, N1s, O1s, S2s and S2p, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum showed four peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.1 eV, and 288.2 eV, representing the C–C/C–N, C–S, and CO, respectively (Fig. 2c). The N1s spectrum in Fig. 2d shows two peaks at 399.5 eV and 401.1 eV, which are related to C–N and N–H, respectively. The high-resolution O1s spectrum revealed three peaks at 531.0 eV and 532.2 eV, corresponding to C–O/C–O–C and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. 2e). For S2p, as illustrated in Fig. 2f, two peaks were observed at 163.0 eV and 164.1 eV, which were regarded as the 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 sites of the -C-S-covalent bond of the thiophene-like S [36,37]. Consequently, the above results demonstrated that the designed CDs contain N and S, which supported the results of the FT-IR analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical performance of the N, S-CDs was investigated by UV–vis and FL spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the UV–vis spectrum presented two absorption bands centered at 215 and 275 nm, which might originate from the π-π∗ transitions of the CC bond and n-π∗ transitions of the CO bond, respectively [20,38]. The designed N, S-CDs displayed a pale yellow color under sunlight and exhibited blue FL emission under 365 nm UV light (inset of Fig. 3a). At the optimal excitation wavelength (λex) of 335 nm, the maximum emission wavelength (λem) was observed at 425 nm, which might be ascribed to the transition of excited electrons from the excited state to the ground state through radiative transitions (Fig. S1) [39,40]. Moreover, the relative quantum yield (QY) was 44.48 %, thus demonstrating the doping of heteroatoms into the N, S-CDs with excellent FL emission. The N, S-CDs revealed an excitation-dependent emission behavior with a redshift of FL emission when the excitation wavelength varied from 300 to 400 nm (Fig. 3b), which may.

be attributed to the irregularity of surface imperfections and quantum confinement effects [17]. Moreover, the as-prepared N, S-CDs also exhibited stronger FL emission under 365 nm UV light in the solid state and the corresponding emission spectra were shown in Fig. S2, the redshifted FL emission of N, S-CDs in the solid phase might be ascribed to the intra- and inter-molecular hydrogenbonding in the aggregated state [41].

To investigate the feasibility of N, S-CDs in practical applications, the stability of the N, S-CDs was evaluated by measuring the FL intensity change under different conditions. As shown in Fig. S3a, the FL intensity at 425 nm reached the maximum value at a pH of 5.0, and it sharply decreased under strongly acidic or alkaline conditions, which was originated from the protonation and deprotonation process of the –NH2 and –OH units [20]. Moreover, the influence of NaCl concentration on the FL emission of N, S-CDs was investigated. As shown in Fig. S3b, the FL intensity at 425 nm for the N, S-CDs was nearly unchanged and decreased by only 6.4 % even when the NaCl concentration increased to 600 mM, demonstrating excellent salt resistance. Additionally, the N, S-CDs showed good photobleaching resistance and the FL intensity slightly decreased to 82.6 % of its initial value even after 30 min of exposure (Fig. S3c). In addition, there was a negligible change in the FL intensity for the N, S-CDs with a long time storage time for 15 days at room temperature, illustrating their good storage stability (Fig. S3d). All of the aforementioned findings declared that the N, S-CDs have excellent stability performance, which offers dependable priority conditions for the design of sensing platforms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FL sensing of TCs by N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized N, S-CDs were applied to detect TCH through a FL sensing method. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the emission spectra of the N, S-CDs suffered from obvious FL quenching in the emission spectra when TCH was gradually increased from 0 to 104.5 μM, and the maximum quenching efficiency ((F0–F)/F0) reached to 82.32 %, where F0 and F were the FL intensities at 425 nm before and after the treatment with TCH, respectively. Fig. 4b showed that two linear relationships were obtained between the values of lg (F0/F) and the TCH concentration in the ranges of 1.66–19.6 μM (R2 = 0.994) and 19.6–104.5 μM (R2 = 0.999), which revealed that the proposed sensor could be used for the quantitative tracing of TCH. The LOD for TCH was determined to be 53.02 nM, which was lower than the maximum acceptable level [17] and was comparable to those of other reported TCH detection sensing platforms based on CD materials (Table S5). Meanwhile, the FL of the N, S-CDs could also be quenched by other TCs including OTC and DOX (Fig. S4 and S5), and the designed sensor could also be used for the quantitative detection of.

OTC and DOX, with a lower LOD for OTC (95.61 nM) and for DOX (41.87 nM). Furthermore, the RSDs of TCH, OTC and DOX were 4.3 %, 3.9 % and 4.6 %, respectively, according to five parallel measurements, indicating excellent reproducibility and accurate determination of the TCs (Fig. S6).

Furthermore, the selectivity of the N, S-CDs for the detection of TCs was evaluated by the introduction of different interferents, including other antibiotics, amino acids and metal ions at a concentration of 104.5 μM. As shown in Fig. 5 , only the TCs (TCH, OTC and DOX) led to the FL quenching, while the other interferences had negligible effects on the FL intensity of the N, S-CDs, which proved that the aforementioned N, S-CDs could be designed as sensitive sensors for the detection of TCs with good anti-interference properties.

Notably, the FL emission of the quenched system (N, S-CDs + TCH) was almost completely recovered via the addition of Al3+. As shown in Fig. 6 a, at an excitation wavelength of 335 nm, the FL intensities of the N, S-CDs gradually increased with a slight redshift upon increasing the amount of Al3+ because of the formation of complexes between TCs and Al3+ ions [25], and the FL recovery reached a maximum of 91.74 % after treatment with Al3+ at a concentration of 104.3 μM. Furthermore, there were two linear relationships between the value of lg (F0/F) and the concentration of Al3+ in the ranges of 3.3–61.4 μM (R2 = 0.999) and 61.4–104.3 μM (R2 = 0.989) (where F0 and F represent the FL intensities of the N, S-CDs and the N, S-CDs + TCH with the addition of different amounts of Al3+), and the LOD of Al3+ based on the above system was determined to be 57.69 nM (Fig. 6b). Moreover, Al3+ could also result in the FL recovery of N, S-CDs that quenched by OTC or DOX, thus an “ON-OFF-ON” fluorescent switch could be constructed successfully (Fig. S7 and S8). In contrast with Al3+, other metal ions could not recover the FL emission of the N, S-CDs quenched by TCH (Fig. S9). The above results revealed that the proposed sensor could also be used for the quantitative tracing of Al3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FL detection mechanism for TCH and Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: The detection mechanisms for FL quenching and the recovery of N, S-CDs caused by TCH and Al3+ were studied systematically. As illustrated in Fig. S10a, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of TCH overlapped with the excitation spectrum of the N, S-CDs, and no additional absorbance peaks formed in the UV–vis absorbance spectrum of N, S-CDs/TCH, thus indicating that the FL quenching might be caused by the inner filter effect (IFE) process [42]. Furthermore, the FL lifetimes of the N, S-CDs solutions with different concentrations of TCH were recorded. As shown in Fig. S10b, the FL lifetime of N, S-CDs was 1.02 ns, and negligible changes occurred upon the treatment with TCH at 50 μM (1.03 ns) and 100 μM (1.02 ns), which revealed that static quenching might have occurred. The Vr and Vnr also remained nearly unchanged after the addition of TCH (Table S6), and the Stern-Volmer equation in Fig. S11 revealed that the quenching constants (Ksv) of TCH, OTC and DOX were 2.68 × 104 M−1, 1.71 × 104 M−1 and 4.13 × 104 M−1, respectively, which were much higher than that of the maximum dynamic quenching, thus confirming that the static quenching process was caused by TCs. Additionally, the Ka values of TCH, OTC and DOX were calculated to be 1.82 × 104 L/mol, 1.91 × 104 L/mol and 4.12 × 104 L/mol, respectively (Fig. S12). On the basis of the above results, it was reasonable to confirm that the actual mechanism of FL quenching for N, S-CDs with the addition of TCs was static quenching along with the IFE.

Moreover, the TEM image after the addition of TCH in Fig. S13a showed that TCH was feasibly absorbed on the surface of the N, S-CDs, thus leading to obvious aggregation of the N, S-CDs. Owing to the greater affinity of Al3+ for TCH than for N, S-CDs, the aggregation of N, S-CDs would be destroyed, and the N, S-CDs would be released to their original state (Fig. S13b), thus causing the overlap region to decrease and causing FL recovery (Fig. S10a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample detection of TCH and Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the feasibility of the designed sensor for practical applications, the detections of TCH and Al3+ in real samples such as tap water and the Songhua River were performed by the standard addition method. The RSD values of TCH detection in tap water sample ranged from 2.69 % to 2.89 %, with recoveries ranging from 98.81 % to 101.84 % (Table S7). Meanwhile, the RSD values of TCH detection in the Songhua River sample ranged from 1.27 % to 3.51 %, and the recovery rates varied from 98.96 % to 101.57 % (Table S8). In addition, different concentrations of Al3+ were introduced to the above N, S-CDs + TCH systems, and the recoveries for Al3+ detection varied from 97.71 % to 103.44 % in tap water and from 98.81 % to 102.87 % for the Songhua River sample. All the RSD values were less than 5 %, which demonstrated the applicability of N, S-CDs for detecting TCH and Al3+ in real samples with excellent accuracy and reliability (Table S9 and S10).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Portable detection of TCH and Al3+ for anti-counterfeiting encryption
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, the as-prepared N, S-CDs hydrogel was used for anti-counterfeiting encryption in a portable manner on the basis of the FL “ON-OFF-ON” strategy. First, different patterns were labeled on the hydrogel rods based on the TCH induced FL quenching process, which could be observed only by irradiation with 365 nm UV light (Fig. 7 ). Interestingly, the above information could be erased after treatment with Al3+ through the FL recovery process without breaking their structure. The above findings indicate the great potential for application in anti-fake information encryption based on N, S-CDs.

The integration of the logic gate operation is highly significant for the construction of smart optoelectronic devices for information storage [43]. Herein, several logic gate operations were designed for the designed N, S-CDs sensor based on the FL “ON-OFF-ON” process, and the N, S-CDs with bright FL emission were used as the initial state. For example, three “NOR” logic gates were developed by using two of the TCs (TCH, OTC and DOX) as the input signals, and the presence or absence of them was determined to be “1” and “0”, respectively. The output for the N, S-CDs with bright FL emission was defined as “1”, and the quenched FL was defined as “0”. As illustrated in Fig. 8 a, the N, S-CDs displayed stronger FL emission (output: 0) without the addition of TCH and OTC (input: 0/0). However, after the addition of TCH or OTC to the N, S-CDs solution, regardless of one or both (inputs: 1/0, 0/1 and 1/1), the above TCs caused FL quenching and the output was “0”. Similarly, another two “NOR” logic gates were developed by using TCH/DOX and OTC/DOX as the input signals, which were illustrated in Fig. S14. Moreover, three kinds of “INHIBIT” logic gate operations were also developed by using Al3+ and one of the TCs (TCH, OTC and DOX) as the input signals. For example, the N, S-CDs displayed a stronger FL emission (output: 1) without adding TCH and Al3+ (input: 0/0). In the presence of TCH alone (input: 1/0), FL quenching of the N, S-CDs occurred, and the output was “0”. Meanwhile, only adding Al3+ (input: 0/1) had little effect on the FL emission of the N, S-CDs, thus the output was “1”. Interestingly, bright FL emission (output: 1) for the N, S-CDs was observed in the presence of both TCH and Al3+

(input: 1/1), which was ascribed to the relatively high affinity between Al3+ and TCH, causing the fortunate FL recovery of the system (Fig. 8b). Another two “INHIBIT” logic gates were developed by using OTC/Al3+ and DOX/Al3+ as the input signals in a similar way (Fig. S15). The above findings may be beneficial for the rational construction of intelligent optical computing systems for information computing.

In summary, an efficient fluorescent sensor of N, S-CDs was designed via the use of L-Cys and CMC polymers as precursors through a facile hydrothermal approach. The designed sensor was utilized for the quantitative tracing of TCs and Al3+ through the FL “ON-OFF-ON” transition strategy with a lower LOD, excellent accuracy, practicability and reliability. Moreover, multiple logical gate operations including “NOR” and “INHIBIT” were carried out on the as-prepared sensor, indicating the potential for the construction of smart optoelectronic devices. In addition, the N, S-CDs could be uploaded to hydrogen bars and used for tracing TCs and Al3+ in a portable manner, which revealed great application in the field of anti-counterfeiting and encryption.

Xiaoxu Cui: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zherui Wan: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation, Formal analysis. Zhonghao Li: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Investigation. Fengxia Wang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision. Pengshuai Wei: Supervision, Formal analysis. Guang Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
The datasets used or analyzed in this work are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that they have no conflicts of interest to this work.
